summary:
The Lascaux cave complex in the Dordogne region of southwestern France contains some of the most spectacular and well-preserved examples of Upper Palaeolithic cave art in the world, dated to approximately 17,000–15,000 BCE. Discovered in 1940 by four teenage boys and their dog, the caves contain over 600 painted and engraved animals as well as geometric designs and rare human figures, covering the walls and ceilings in vivid ochre, manganese dioxide, charcoal, and clay pigments applied by Magdalenian artists.

The animal paintings at Lascaux depict horses, aurochs, stags, bison, bears, rhinoceroses, and lions with a dynamism and skill that astonished the world. The Hall of the Bulls, with its large composite paintings of aurochs and other animals, is among the most celebrated prehistoric artworks ever discovered. The famous "Shaft" scene depicts a wounded bison and a bird-headed man lying beside a broken spear — one of the rare narrative or mythological scenes in Palaeolithic art, whose interpretation remains actively debated.

Lascaux was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979. The original caves were closed to the public in 1963 after atmospheric changes caused by visitor breath damaged the paintings with algae and mold, and visitors now tour the exceptionally accurate replica Lascaux II (and the recently opened Lascaux IV) nearby. Lascaux predates the period of this era (10,000–8,000 BCE) but is included in the registry as a prominent cultural marker; by the time of this era, the tradition of Magdalenian cave painting in southwestern France had ended, likely with the cultural changes accompanying the [[event:1262|end of the Ice Age]] and the subsequent [[event:1280|Azilian transition]].
